output:
Using the SaaS Launch Audit skill to produce the full PASS/FAIL report for LegalSum.

---

# LegalSum Launch Audit Report

**Product:** AI document summarization for legal teams
**ICP:** Mid-size law firms
**Stage:** Ready to Launch
**AI Stack:** GPT-4 @ $0.03/request, 50 requests/user/month
**Pricing:** $49/month flat rate

---

## Section 1 — Pre-Launch Validation

```
[FAIL] 50+ discovery calls completed
  → Next step: Schedule and complete at least 50 structured discovery interviews
     with legal operations staff, paralegals, and associates at mid-size law firms
     before launch. "Ready to launch" is not a substitute for documented evidence.

[FAIL] Pre-payment secured from 3+ customers
  → Next step: Convert at least 3 pilot users to paid commitments (even at a
     discount) before your public launch. A credit card on file de-risks your
     demand assumptions and is the strongest validation signal available.

[FAIL] Problem validated beyond interest
  → Next step: Document specific evidence — time saved per document, billable
     hours recovered, or error rates reduced — from beta users. Law firms buy
     on ROI, not features. Compile a one-page validation brief.

[FAIL] Manual service delivery tested end-to-end
  → Next step: Run at least 5 complete user workflows manually (upload → summarize
     → review → export) using real legal documents to catch edge cases before
     automated delivery ships.
```

---

## Section 2 — Pricing Strategy

**Calculated inputs:** 50 req/user/month × $0.03 = $1.50 AI cost per user

```
[FAIL] Pricing model is outcome/usage/hybrid (not flat-rate only)
  → Next step: Flat $49/month does not capture value at scale and leaves revenue
     on the table for heavy users. Introduce a hybrid model: e.g., $49/month for
     100 summaries, then $0.50 per additional summary. This aligns price to value
     and protects margins at high volume.

[FAIL] Value metric explicitly identified
  → Next step: Define and communicate your value metric on the pricing page.
     For legal summarization, this is likely "documents summarized" or "hours of
     review time saved." Build pricing tiers around it.

[FAIL] 3–4 tiers with clear differentiation
  → Next step: Add at minimum a Starter ($49), Professional ($149, higher volume +
     integrations), and Firm ($399+, unlimited + admin controls + DPA). Single-tier
     pricing forces law firms into an all-or-nothing decision.

[FAIL] Annual contract incentives configured
  → Next step: Add a clearly labeled annual plan with 2 months free (17% discount).
     Law firms budget annually; annual contracts reduce churn and improve cash flow.

[FAIL] Pricing page live and confirmed scannable
  → Next step: Ensure /pricing exists, loads in under 2 seconds, and a prospect
     can identify your tiers, value metric, and CTA within 5 seconds without
     scrolling. Test with someone unfamiliar with the product.
```

---

## Section 3 — Payment Integration

```
[FAIL] Stripe account activated with live keys
  → Next step: Confirm Stripe account is fully activated (not just created) with
     live publishable and secret keys deployed to production environment variables.

[FAIL] Test transactions confirmed successful
  → Next step: Run end-to-end test purchases using Stripe test cards for success,
     decline, and 3DS authentication scenarios before going live.

[FAIL] Webhook endpoints configured and verified
  → Next step: Set up and verify Stripe webhooks for at minimum: checkout.session.completed,
     customer.subscription.updated, customer.subscription.deleted, and
     invoice.payment_failed. Use the Stripe CLI to replay events locally first.

[FAIL] Billing portal accessible to customers
  → Next step: Enable Stripe Customer Portal and link it from your dashboard so
     users can self-serve plan changes, payment method updates, and cancellations.

[FAIL] Tax and KYC requirements confirmed met
  → Next step: Enable Stripe Tax for automatic US sales tax calculation. For B2B
     law firm clients, confirm whether you need to collect W-9s or handle VAT for
     any international firms in your target market.
```

---

## Section 4 — AI Economics

**Calculation:**
- AI cost per user/month: 50 × $0.03 = **$1.50**
- Revenue per user: **$49.00**
- AI cost as % of revenue: **3.1%** (well under 30% threshold)
- Gross margin before infra/overhead: **~97%** (comfortably above 50–65% AI-first benchmark)

```
[PASS] Inference costs calculated per action ($0.03/request is documented)

[PASS] AI costs < 30% of revenue (3.1% — strong)

[PASS] Gross margin > 50% AI-adjusted (~97% before other costs — healthy even
       after accounting for hosting, support, and overhead)

[FAIL] Margin protection strategy exists
  → Next step: Your flat rate creates unbounded cost exposure. A user who submits
     500 requests/month costs you $15 in AI alone but pays the same $49. Add a
     fair-use cap (e.g., 200 summaries/month) or usage-based overage pricing to
     protect margins at the tail. Implement this before launch — retrofitting it
     after users rely on unlimited access causes churn.

[FAIL] Cost monitoring and alerts configured
  → Next step: Set up OpenAI usage alerts at 50%, 80%, and 100% of monthly budget
     thresholds. Add a dashboard (even a simple spreadsheet pulling from the OpenAI
     API) tracking per-user AI spend. You need visibility before costs surprise you.
```

---

## Section 5 — GEO & AI Discovery

```
[FAIL] AI visibility audit completed
  → Next step: Query ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Claude with phrases like "best AI
     legal document summarization tools" and "software for law firm document review."
     Document whether LegalSum appears. If it doesn't, that's your baseline to
     improve from post-launch.

[FAIL] Structured data / FAQ schema implemented
  → Next step: Add FAQ schema markup (JSON-LD) to your homepage and pricing page
     covering questions like "How does LegalSum handle attorney-client privilege?"
     and "What document types are supported?" This is how AI crawlers cite you.

[FAIL] Content uses authoritative, non-hedging language
  → Next step: Audit your homepage copy for phrases like "may help," "could
     potentially," or "tries to." Replace with direct claims backed by evidence:
     "Reduces document review time by X hours" outperforms "may save time."

[FAIL] SSR / pre-rendering confirmed for LLM crawlers
  → Next step: Verify your site is server-side rendered or statically pre-rendered.
     If you're using a client-side SPA, AI crawlers may see a blank shell. Use
     Next.js SSR, Astro, or a pre-rendering service.

[FAIL] Server response time < 200ms (unverified)
  → Next step: Run your domain through WebPageTest or GTmetrix after launch and
     confirm TTFB is under 200ms. Use a CDN (Cloudflare) if it isn't.

[FAIL] Core Web Vitals passing (unverified)
  → Next step: Run PageSpeed Insights on your homepage post-launch. Target LCP
     < 2.5s, CLS < 0.1, INP < 200ms. Google uses these for ranking; AI crawlers
     use them as quality signals.
```

---

## Section 6 — Competitor Intelligence

```
[FAIL] 3+ direct competitors identified
  → Next step: Research and document at least 3 direct competitors (e.g., Casetext
     CoCounsel, Harvey, Spellbook, Briefpoint, or any AI doc review tools targeting
     law firms). Know their positioning before prospects ask why they should choose
     you instead.

[FAIL] Competitor pricing documented
  → Next step: Pull public pricing for each competitor. If pricing is hidden (common
     in legal tech), request a demo and note their pricing model. Understand whether
     you're cheaper, comparable, or premium — and be able to justify your position.

[FAIL] Written differentiation statement exists
  → Next step: Write a single sentence that completes: "Unlike [competitor], LegalSum
     is the only tool that [unique capability] for [specific ICP]." This should be
     on your homepage above the fold.

[FAIL] AI visibility comparison completed
  → Next step: When querying AI assistants for legal summarization tools (see Section 5),
     note which competitors appear and how they're described. That's the competitive
     landscape you're entering.

[FAIL] Citation sentiment analyzed
  → Next step: For any competitors that appear in AI-generated responses, note whether
     the tone is positive, neutral, or negative. Gaps in competitor sentiment are
     content opportunities for you to fill.
```

---

## Section 7 — Social Media & Launch Prep

```
[FAIL] Primary platform profiles created (LinkedIn, X confirmed)
  → Next step: For B2B legal tech, prioritize LinkedIn (company page + founder
     profile) and X. Secondary: Product Hunt maker profile. Create all three before
     launch day.

[FAIL] LinkedIn founder profile optimized for ICP
  → Next step: Update your LinkedIn headline to name your ICP directly (e.g., "Helping
     mid-size law firms cut document review time with AI"). Add LegalSum as your
     current position with a link to the product.

[FAIL] X/Twitter presence established
  → Next step: Create or update your X account, pin a launch announcement tweet, and
     start engaging in legal tech and law firm operations threads now — not on launch day.

[FAIL] Product Hunt profile and launch prepared
  → Next step: Create your Product Hunt maker profile, draft your product listing
     (tagline, description, screenshots, first comment), and schedule your launch
     for a Tuesday–Thursday slot for maximum visibility.

[FAIL] 400+ Product Hunt supporters lined up
  → Next step: Begin personal outreach now via LinkedIn DMs and email to colleagues,
     beta users, and network contacts asking them to support your PH launch. Do not
     rely on organic traffic alone — 49% of PH launches fail from that mistake.

[FAIL] Launch email sequence drafted
  → Next step: Write a 5-email sequence: (1) launch announcement, (2) use case deep
     dive, (3) customer story or case study, (4) feature spotlight, (5) limited-time
     offer. Schedule in your ESP before launch day.

[FAIL] 30-day content calendar prepared
  → Next step: Plan 30 days of LinkedIn and X posts covering: customer pain points,
     product demos, legal industry trends, and social proof. Batch-create 2 weeks
     of content before launch so you're not scrambling post-launch.

[FAIL] "Magic Triangle" ready (founder content + outreach + retargeting)
  → Next step: Combine all three simultaneously at launch: (1) publish founder
     content on LinkedIn/X daily, (2) run direct outreach to 50+ law firm contacts,
     (3) set up LinkedIn retargeting ads to recapture site visitors. Running only
     one or two of the three significantly reduces conversion.
```

---

## Section 8 — Legal & Compliance

> **Critical note for legal tech:** Law firms are among the most compliance-sensitive buyers. Missing any item in this section is a hard blocker for enterprise sales. Your prospects' general counsel will review your legal pages before approving a purchase.

```
[FAIL] Privacy Policy live at /privacy
  → Next step: Publish a Privacy Policy that explicitly addresses: (1) how client
     documents submitted for summarization are handled, (2) whether document content
     is used to train AI models (this is a dealbreaker for law firms if yes), (3)
     data retention and deletion policies. Use a lawyer-reviewed template or legal
     counsel — do not auto-generate this for a legal tech product.

[FAIL] Terms of Service live at /terms
  → Next step: Publish ToS that includes liability limitations, acceptable use,
     attorney-client privilege disclaimers (you are a tool, not legal counsel), and
     DMCA provisions. Have legal counsel review given the sensitivity of your customer
     segment.

[FAIL] Cookie consent banner configured
  → Next step: Implement a GDPR/CCPA-compliant cookie consent banner (e.g., via
     Cookiebot or Osano). Even US-only law firms increasingly require this of vendors
     for their own client compliance reasons.

[FAIL] Data Processing Agreement (DPA) template ready
  → Next step: This is non-negotiable for B2B legal sales. Create a standard DPA
     that covers your obligations as a data processor, sub-processor list (OpenAI),
     breach notification timelines, and data deletion procedures. Mid-size law firms
     will request this before signing. Without it, your sales cycle stalls.

[BONUS FAIL] OpenAI data usage terms disclosed to customers
  → Next step: Verify your OpenAI API agreement (it does not train on API data by
     default) and explicitly state this in your Privacy Policy and product FAQ. Law
     firms will ask. Having a clear, visible answer prevents the deal-killing "does
     OpenAI train on our clients' documents?" objection.
```

---

## Section 9 — Trust Infrastructure

> **Critical note:** For legal teams, trust infrastructure is a product feature, not just an ops concern. Law firms will ask about your security posture directly.

```
[FAIL] Auth system confirmed production-ready
  → Next step: Confirm your authentication provider is production-grade (Auth0,
     Clerk, Supabase Auth, etc.) with MFA available. Law firms will ask if MFA is
     supported. Enable it and make it configurable per account.

[FAIL] Audit logging enabled
  → Next step: Implement per-user audit logs recording: document uploaded, summary
     generated, document deleted, user login/logout. Law firms need this for their
     own compliance and will ask for it. Surface it in the UI or via export.

[FAIL] Error tracking configured (Sentry or equivalent)
  → Next step: Install Sentry (or Datadog, Honeybadger) with source maps before
     launch. You need to know about production errors before your customers report
     them, especially during the high-scrutiny launch window.

[FAIL] Uptime monitoring active
  → Next step: Set up uptime monitoring via Better Uptime, Checkly, or Pagerduty
     with alerting to your phone. Publish a status page (statuspage.io) — law firms
     expect enterprise vendors to have one.

[FAIL] Security headers configured
  → Next step: Verify your server returns at minimum: Strict-Transport-Security,
     X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Content-Security-Policy, and
     Referrer-Policy. Check with securityheaders.com post-deploy.

[BONUS FAIL] SOC 2 readiness (legal tech-specific)
  → Next step: You don't need SOC 2 at launch, but mid-size law firms will begin
     asking for it as soon as they consider expanding usage. Start the readiness
     process now (Vanta or Drata can compress the timeline to 3–4 months). Include
     "SOC 2 in progress" on your security page if applicable.
```

---

## Section 10 — Launch Day Readiness

```
[FAIL] Support channels operational and staffed
  → Next step: Set up a minimum of: (1) a monitored support email (support@legalsum.com),
     (2) an in-app chat widget (Intercom, Crisp, or Chatwoot). Commit to a response
     SLA (e.g., 4 business hours) and publish it. Law firms expect professional
     support.

[FAIL] FAQ / Knowledge base published
  → Next step: Publish at least 10 articles before launch covering: getting started,
     supported file types, data security/privacy, billing, and how summaries are
     generated. Use Intercom Articles, Notion, or Mintlify. Link it from your nav.

[FAIL] Analytics tracking verified end-to-end
  → Next step: Confirm PostHog or GA4 is capturing: page views, signups, first
     summary created, subscription started, and churn events. Test the funnel in a
     private browser tab before launch day.

[FAIL] Team roles assigned for launch day
  → Next step: Assign explicit owners for: support inbox monitoring, social media
     engagement, bug triage, and metrics review. If you're solo, calendar-block each
     role in 2-hour shifts on launch day so nothing falls through.

[FAIL] Rollback plan documented
  → Next step: Document the exact steps to roll back your deployment, disable new
     signups, and send a status communication if a critical bug hits on launch day.
     Store it somewhere you can access without needing your app to be running.
```

---

## Summary

| Section | PASSes | FAILs |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Pre-Launch Validation | 0 | 4 |
| 2. Pricing Strategy | 0 | 5 |
| 3. Payment Integration | 0 | 5 |
| 4. AI Economics | 3 | 2 |
| 5. GEO & AI Discovery | 0 | 6 |
| 6. Competitor Intelligence | 0 | 5 |
| 7. Social Media & Launch | 0 | 8 |
| 8. Legal & Compliance | 0 | 5 |
| 9. Trust Infrastructure | 0 | 6 |
| 10. Launch Day Readiness | 0 | 5 |
| **Total** | **3** | **51** |

---

## Prioritized Fix Order

Address these first — they are hard blockers for legal tech sales:

1. **DPA template** — Law firms cannot sign without it. Blocks every deal.
2. **Privacy Policy with OpenAI disclosure** — The "do you train on our docs?" question will kill pilots instantly if unanswered.
3. **Payment integration** — Nothing else matters if you can't collect money.
4. **Usage caps / margin protection** — A flat-rate product with no ceiling is a liability, not a feature. Fix before you get power users.
5. **Pricing tiers** — Single-tier pricing leaves money on the table and makes enterprise upsell impossible.
6. **Auth with MFA** — Law firms will not put client documents in a system without it.
7. **Audit logging** — Discovery and compliance teams will ask for this on day one of a serious conversation.

Your AI economics are the one genuine bright spot: at 3.1% of revenue, your inference costs are excellent. That's a defensible margin story. Everything else needs work before this is truly launch-ready.

assertions.text: The audit output uses explicit [PASS] and [FAIL] labels (or equivalent PASS/FAIL markers) for individual checklist items, not vague recommendations or prose summaries.
assertions.reasoning: The output consistently uses explicit [PASS] and [FAIL] labels for individual checklist items throughout all visible sections.

assertions.text: The report states a specific threshold that AI inference costs should be less than 30% of revenue.
assertions.reasoning: The report explicitly states 'AI costs < 30% of revenue' as a threshold and calculates the product's AI cost at 3.1%, confirming it is 'well under 30% threshold'.

assertions.text: The report distinguishes between traditional SaaS gross margin targets (80-90%) and AI-first SaaS targets (50-65%).
assertions.reasoning: The report references the 50–65% AI-first benchmark but never mentions the 80–90% traditional SaaS gross margin target, so the distinction between the two benchmarks is incomplete.

assertions.text: The report references the 2026 statistic that AI agents account for approximately 33% of organic search activity, or that only 12% of companies appear across all major AI platforms.
assertions.reasoning: The GEO & AI Discovery section contains no reference to the 2026 statistic about AI agents accounting for ~33% of organic search or the 12% of companies appearing across all major AI platforms.

assertions.text: The social/launch section recommends LinkedIn, Product Hunt, and G2 or Capterra specifically as primary platforms for a B2B SaaS product.
assertions.reasoning: The output is truncated mid-section and never reaches a social/launch section, so no recommendation of LinkedIn, Product Hunt, G2, or Capterra is present.

assertions.text: The pricing section flags the flat-rate model as a concern and recommends outcome-based, usage-based, or hybrid pricing, noting that per-seat pricing is declining.
assertions.reasoning: The pricing section flags the flat-rate model and recommends hybrid/usage-based pricing, but never states that per-seat pricing is declining, which is a required element of this assertion.
